observations.polarity: Life is vibrant and colorful vs. Death is decay and silence
observations.summary: The passage contrasts the vibrant, colorful threads that once symbolized life and beauty with the decayed, rotting environment symbolizing death and the cessation of life.

left_evidence.quote: Every color under heaven woven together in patterns that had made people stop in the street and stare.

right_evidence.quote: Rotting textiles—Zuna's sacred threads left mid-shuttle—had unraveled into mildew and draped over discarded animal bones, making a dry rustling sound when disturbed that was the sound of all life giving up its last argument with death.

observations.polarity: Desire to breathe vs. desire to stop breathing
observations.summary: The environment is described as designed to make a person stop wanting to breathe, contrasting the natural act of breathing with an oppressive atmosphere that suffocates.

left_evidence.quote: The smell of the place was a terrible architecture—designed, it seemed, by someone who had thought very carefully about what it would take to make a person stop wanting to breathe.

right_evidence.quote: The patient mineral cold of turned earth. The still patience of serpents in a garden.

observations.polarity: All colors embraced vs. Certain colors forbidden
observations.summary: While the threads once included every color under heaven, three colors were never touched by Zuna, symbolizing a sacred exclusion and mystery.

left_evidence.quote: Every color under heaven woven together in patterns that had made people stop in the street and stare.

right_evidence.quote: Every color, that is, except the three she never touched—never blue, never purple, never scarlet—and she had never explained why.

accepted_observations.summary: The environment is described as designed to make a person stop wanting to breathe, contrasting the natural act of breathing with an oppressive atmosphere that suffocates.
